Text: Wow this was a movie was completely captivating I could not believe that I started awake so late to watch it but it came on late ounce I started watching I couldn't stop it had a full range of very good cast members wow even Eartha Kitt and Ruby Dee Forrest Whittaker and James Earl Jones and many mo...
In [ ]:
def check_text_lstm(text, model, stoi, seq_len):
    # same preprocessing as Part A
    encoded_review = [stoi[word] for word in preprocess_sentence(text) if word in stoi]
    padded_review = padding_([encoded_review], seq_len)
    review_tensor = torch.from_numpy(padded_review).long().to(DEVICE)

    model.eval()
    with torch.no_grad():
        hidden = model.init_hidden(1)
        output, _ = model(review_tensor, hidden)
        prob = output.item()

    pred = "Positive" if prob >= 0.5 else "Negative"
    return pred, prob



# Take the first real LSTM error sample
lstm_raw = lstm_errs[0][0]

# Make a small edit to strengthen the negative sentiment
lstm_modified = lstm_raw.replace("a little disappointed", "very disappointed")

print("LSTM MODIFY TEST")
print("Original text (first 300 chars):")
print(lstm_raw[:300], "...\n")

print("Modified text (first 300 chars):")
print(lstm_modified[:300], "...\n")

print("Original prediction:", check_text_lstm(lstm_raw, lstm_model, stoi, SEQ_LEN))
print("Modified prediction:", check_text_lstm(lstm_modified, lstm_model, stoi, SEQ_LEN))

In [ ]:
'''
PROVIDE YOUR ANSWER BELOW


The errors made by BERT and LSTM differ in quality as well. Even BERT has fewer errors, the errors are more nuanced.
For instance, in the first review, the text starts off with a negative comment (bums me out), but it is clear that the
review itself is positive. The first comment is actually toward the movie dvd, instead of the movie itself.
The second review that was misclassified was written ironic style. The sentiment is expressed indirectly
through contrast and sarcasm rather than through explicit negative words, which can easily confuse the model.


The LSTM errors are more easier to explain. In several false positives, the review is
overall negative but contains scattered positive phrases such as "some of the acting is good" or
"one cool thing." The LSTM appears to over-weight these local positive cues. Its false negatives also
show that it struggles with long, noisy, run-on reviews or reviews that contain many negative topic words
even when the final opinion is positive. This is consistent with the known weakness of LSTMs on long-range
dependencies and mixed sentiment.

I also tried making small edits to one misclassified review for each model to see whether the prediction
could be corrected while keeping the text close to the original. For the LSTM example, changing "a little
disappointed" to "very disappointed" reduced the positive probability from 0.6773 to 0.6492, but the
model still predicted positive. For the BERT example, adding a stronger positive phrase increased the
score from 0.0089 to 0.0415, but it still predicted negative. So the edits pushed the confidence in the
expected direction, but they were not sufficient to flip the label.

This suggests that the errors are not caused by only one isolated word. Instead, they depend on the broader
context and the overall structure of the review. A successful correction would likely require slightly larger
edits, such as strengthening the final recommendation or removing more of the mixed-sentiment wording, but that
would make the edited review less faithful to the original text.




'''

Part (iv) [2pt DISCUSSION]¶

Find three positive (i.e., 5-star) and three negative (i.e., 1-star or 1/2-star) audience reviews for the movie Captain America: Brave New World (2025) from Rotten Tomatoes. Evaluate these six new reviews with your best BERT and LSTM models from parts A and B. Present your findings in a Pandas DataFrame with four columns for the review text, actual sentiment, LSTM prediction, and BERT prediction. After displaying the DataFrame, discuss your results: How well do they perform? Compare the two models and analyze any specific reviews that were predicted incorrectly.

In [ ]:
# TO BE COMPLETED

reviews = [
    "Well Red Hulk was cool and that’s about it",
    "I hated this movie so bad",
    "Ass it sucked so bad I want my money back",
    "I liked that there was a buildup to the story line. Didn’t like that they changed out the general for Harrison Ford.",
    "It was amazing movie!",
    "Loved Sam!!! Funny and action packed"
]

actual_sentiments = [
    "negative",
    "negative",
    "negative",
    "positive",
    "positive",
    "positive"
]
In [ ]:
#BRET Prediction

import torch

def predict_bert(text, model, tokenizer, max_len=400):
    model.eval()

    enc = tokenizer(
        text,
        padding='max_length',
        max_length=max_len,
        truncation=True,
        return_attention_mask=True,
        return_tensors='pt'
    ).to(DEVICE)

    with torch.no_grad():
        prob = model(enc['input_ids'], enc['attention_mask']).item()

    pred = "positive" if prob >= 0.5 else "negative"
    return pred, prob
In [ ]:
#LSTM Prediction
def predict_lstm(text):
    lstm_model.eval()

    encoded_review = [stoi[word] for word in preprocess_sentence(text) if word in stoi]
    padded_review = padding_([encoded_review], SEQ_LEN)
    review_tensor = torch.from_numpy(padded_review).long().to(DEVICE)

    with torch.no_grad():
        hidden = lstm_model.init_hidden(1)
        output, _ = lstm_model(review_tensor, hidden)
        prob = output.item()

    pred = "positive" if prob >= 0.5 else "negative"
    return pred, prob
In [ ]:
rows = []

for review, label in zip(reviews, actual_sentiments):
    # Pass the required model and tokenizer to predict_bert
    lstm_pred, lstm_prob = predict_lstm(review)
    bert_pred, bert_prob = predict_bert(review, bert_model, tokenizer)

    rows.append({
        "Review Text": review,
        "Actual Sentiment": label,
        "LSTM Prediction": lstm_pred,
        "BERT Prediction": bert_pred,
        "LSTM Prob": round(lstm_prob, 4),
        "BERT Prob": round(bert_prob, 4)
    })

# Results
df_rt_compare = pd.DataFrame(rows)
display(df_rt_compare)
Review Text Actual Sentiment LSTM Prediction BERT Prediction LSTM Prob BERT Prob
0 Well Red Hulk was cool and that’s about it negative positive negative 0.6106 0.4431
1 I hated this movie so bad negative negative negative 0.4106 0.0236
2 Ass it sucked so bad I want my money back negative negative negative 0.2852 0.0678
3 I liked that there was a buildup to the story ... positive positive negative 0.6812 0.2122
4 It was amazing movie! positive positive positive 0.7648 0.9794
5 Loved Sam!!! Funny and action packed positive positive positive 0.7352 0.9906
In [ ]:
'''
PROVIDE YOUR ANSWER BELOW

From these six Rotten Tomatoes audience reviews, both models got 5 out of 6 correct, but
they made mistake on different examples.

The LSTM misclassified the negative review "Well Red Hulk was cool and that's about it" as positive,
probably because it over-weighted the word "cool" and did not fully capture the dismissive ending.
BERT handled that review correctly.


BERT misclassified the mixed positive review
"I liked that there was a buildup to the story line. Didn't like that they changed out the general for Harrison Ford."
as negative. This makes sense because the review contains both positive and negative description,
and the negative description may have dominated the final prediction. However, the LSTM labeled that
example correctly.

Both models were very confident on the short, strongly worded reviews such as "I hated this
movie so bad" and "Loved Sam!!! Funny and action packed." That suggests the main difficulty
is not short length itself, but mixed sentiment and informal phrasing. These Rotten Tomatoes
reviews are also shorter and more slang-heavy than the IMDB reviews, so there is some data
shift. Overall, BERT seems slightly better calibrated on clear negatives, while the LSTM can
be pulled toward a positive prediction by a single favorable word or phrase.




'''

PART C (Optional) - Bonus Challenge!¶

This is an optional exercise for those that finish the assignment early and would like to take on a challenging task.

In part A we constructed and trained an LSTM model to identify the sentiment in movie reviews. In Part B we used the embeddings of a BERT model pretrained on a large corpus of text to demonstrate how transfer learning can be used to improve our movie sentiment model. The BERT model is one of many language models that we could have used to implement transfer learning.

For this bonus challenge you are asked to implement a generative character-level LSTM model to produce IMDB movie reviews. Once the model is sufficiently trained you can then use its hidden states as the embedding for training a movie sentiment model. Construct your new movie sentiment analysis model and compare the performance against the model from part A and B.

There are many variants of a generative LSTM model that you can consider. As a starting point you can use the generative LSTM sample code provided in the lecture notes. Specifically, the one used to generate Shakeaspeare. More advanced versions of a generative LSTM can be found in the Universal Language Model Fine-turing for Text Classification (ULMfit) paper (https://arxiv.org/abs/1801.06146).

Tasks:

  1. Create a generative character-level LSTM model trained to create IMDB reviews
  2. Create a classifier using the embeddings from the generative LSTM model (from step 1) to identify positive and negative reviews.
  3. Compare the performance of the model with the results in parts A and B of the assignment.
  4. Upgrade the generative LSTM model using the techniques listed in the ULMfit paper (e.g., bi-directional LSTM, pretraining with wikipedia text and fine-tuning on IMDBT reviews, etc.).

Bonus marks will be provided based on the number of steps completed. Summarize below your results and anything intersting you learned from the steps that you completed. Bonus marks cannot be accumulated beyond a maximum assignment grade.

In [7]:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import numpy as np

# Grab a subset of text
text = " ".join(X_train[:1000])
chars = tuple(set(text))
int2char = dict(enumerate(chars))
char2int = {c: i for i, c in int2char.items()}
encoded = np.array([char2int[c] for c in text])

def get_batches(arr, batch_size, seq_len):
    total_size = batch_size * seq_len
    n_batches = len(arr) // total_size
    arr = arr[:n_batches * total_size].reshape((batch_size, -1))

    for n in range(0, arr.shape[1], seq_len):
        x = arr[:, n:n+seq_len]
        y = np.zeros_like(x)
        try:
            y[:, :-1], y[:, -1] = x[:, 1:], arr[:, n+seq_len]
        except IndexError:
            y[:, :-1], y[:, -1] = x[:, 1:], arr[:, 0]
        yield x, y

class CharLSTM(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, chars, hidden_dim=256, num_layers=2, drop_p=0.5):
        super().__init__()
        self.chars = chars
        self.hidden_dim = hidden_dim
        self.num_layers = num_layers

        self.emb = nn.Embedding(len(chars), hidden_dim)
        self.lstm = nn.LSTM(hidden_dim, hidden_dim, num_layers, dropout=drop_p, batch_first=True)
        self.dropout = nn.Dropout(drop_p)
        self.fc = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, len(chars))

    def forward(self, x, hidden):
        x = self.emb(x)
        out, hidden = self.lstm(x, hidden)
        out = self.dropout(out)
        out = self.fc(out.reshape(-1, self.hidden_dim))
        return out, hidden

    def init_hidden(self, batch_size):
        weight = next(self.parameters()).data
        return (weight.new(self.num_layers, batch_size, self.hidden_dim).zero_().to(DEVICE),
                weight.new(self.num_layers, batch_size, self.hidden_dim).zero_().to(DEVICE))

# Initialize
char_model = CharLSTM(chars).to(DEVICE)
print(f"Vocab size: {len(chars)} | Total chars: {len(encoded)}")

# Quick training loop
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(char_model.parameters(), lr=0.001)
criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()

epochs = 5
batch_size = 128
seq_len = 100

print("Starting Char-LSTM training...")
char_model.train()
for e in range(epochs):
    h = char_model.init_hidden(batch_size)
    for x, y in get_batches(encoded, batch_size, seq_len):
        x = torch.from_numpy(x).long().to(DEVICE)
        y = torch.from_numpy(y).long().to(DEVICE)

        h = tuple([each.data for each in h])
        optimizer.zero_grad()

        output, h = char_model(x, h)
        loss = criterion(output, y.view(-1))
        loss.backward()
        nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(char_model.parameters(), 5)
        optimizer.step()

    print(f"Epoch {e+1}/{epochs} | Loss: {loss.item():.4f}")

Vocab size: 119 | Total chars: 1258895
Starting Char-LSTM training...
Epoch 1/5 | Loss: 2.5140
Epoch 2/5 | Loss: 2.2516
Epoch 3/5 | Loss: 2.1186
Epoch 4/5 | Loss: 2.0408
Epoch 5/5 | Loss: 1.9765
In [8]:
# Classifier using pretrained Char-LSTM embeddings
class CharSentimentClassifier(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, pretrained_model, hidden_dim=256, num_layers=2, drop_p=0.5):
        super().__init__()
        self.hidden_dim = hidden_dim
        self.num_layers = num_layers

        # Reuse embeddings and LSTM from the generative model
        self.emb = pretrained_model.emb
        self.lstm = pretrained_model.lstm

        # Freeze pretrained layers to use as a pure feature extractor
        for param in self.emb.parameters():
            param.requires_grad = False
        for param in self.lstm.parameters():
            param.requires_grad = False

        self.dropout = nn.Dropout(drop_p)
        self.fc = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, 1)
        self.sig = nn.Sigmoid()

    def forward(self, x, hidden):
        x = self.emb(x)
        out, hidden = self.lstm(x, hidden)

        # Take the output of the last sequence step for classification
        out = self.dropout(out[:, -1, :])
        out = self.fc(out)
        return self.sig(out), hidden

    def init_hidden(self, batch_size):
        weight = next(self.parameters()).data
        return (weight.new(self.num_layers, batch_size, self.hidden_dim).zero_().to(DEVICE),
                weight.new(self.num_layers, batch_size, self.hidden_dim).zero_().to(DEVICE))

# Initialize the transfer learning classifier
char_classifier = CharSentimentClassifier(char_model).to(DEVICE)
print("Transfer Learning Classifier Architecture:")
print(char_classifier)

Transfer Learning Classifier Architecture:
CharSentimentClassifier(
  (emb): Embedding(119, 256)
  (lstm): LSTM(256, 256, num_layers=2, batch_first=True, dropout=0.5)
  (dropout): Dropout(p=0.5, inplace=False)
  (fc): Linear(in_features=256, out_features=1, bias=True)
  (sig): Sigmoid()
)
In [9]:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

CHAR_SEQ_LEN = 500

def text_to_char_ids(texts, seq_len):
    features = np.zeros((len(texts), seq_len), dtype=int)
    for i, txt in enumerate(texts):
        encoded = [char2int.get(c, 0) for c in txt]
        if len(encoded) > 0:
            features[i, -len(encoded):] = np.array(encoded)[:seq_len]
    return features

# Process data
char_train_x = text_to_char_ids(X_train, CHAR_SEQ_LEN)
char_val_x = text_to_char_ids(X_val, CHAR_SEQ_LEN)
char_test_x = text_to_char_ids(X_test, CHAR_SEQ_LEN)

char_train_loader, char_val_loader, char_test_loader = New_DataLoader(
    char_train_x, train_y, char_val_x, val_y, char_test_x, test_y, batch_size=128
)

# Train classifier head
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(filter(lambda p: p.requires_grad, char_classifier.parameters()), lr=0.005)
criterion = nn.BCELoss()

char_classifier.train()
for x, y in char_train_loader:
    x, y = x.to(DEVICE), y.to(DEVICE).float()
    h = char_classifier.init_hidden(x.size(0))
    out, _ = char_classifier(x, h)

    loss = criterion(out.squeeze(), y)
    optimizer.zero_grad()
    loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()

# Evaluation
def get_char_acc(model, loader):
    model.eval()
    correct, total = 0, 0
    with torch.no_grad():
        for x, y in loader:
            x, y = x.to(DEVICE), y.to(DEVICE).float()
            h = model.init_hidden(x.size(0))
            out, _ = model(x, h)
            preds = torch.round(out.squeeze())
            correct += preds.eq(y).sum().item()
            total += y.size(0)
    return correct / total

char_test_acc = get_char_acc(char_classifier, char_test_loader)

# Comparison
df_compare = pd.DataFrame({
    "Model": ["Word-level LSTM (Part A)", "BERT (Part B)", "Char-level LSTM (Part C)"],
    "Test Accuracy": [0.8496, 0.9275, char_test_acc]
})

display(df_compare)
Model Test Accuracy
0 Word-level LSTM (Part A) 0.8496
1 BERT (Part B) 0.9275
2 Char-level LSTM (Part C) 0.5020
In [10]:
# Upgrade 1: Unfreeze pretrained LSTM
from copy import deepcopy

def set_requires_grad(module, flag):
    for param in module.parameters():
        param.requires_grad = flag

def count_trainable_params(model):
    return sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters() if p.requires_grad)

# Keep the original Part C model untouched and make a separate upgrade copy
char_model_upgrade = deepcopy(char_model).to(DEVICE)
for param in char_model_upgrade.parameters():
    param.requires_grad = True

print("Upgrade backbone copied and unfrozen.")
print("Trainable parameters in upgraded LM:", count_trainable_params(char_model_upgrade))

Upgrade backbone copied and unfrozen.
Trainable parameters in upgraded LM: 1113719
In [11]:
# Bonus Upgrade 2: Concat pooling classifier
class CharSentimentClassifierConcat(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, pretrained_model, hidden_dim=256, num_layers=2, drop_p=0.5, freeze_backbone=False):
        super().__init__()
        self.hidden_dim = hidden_dim
        self.num_layers = num_layers

        self.emb = pretrained_model.emb
        self.lstm = pretrained_model.lstm

        set_requires_grad(self.emb, not freeze_backbone)
        set_requires_grad(self.lstm, not freeze_backbone)

        self.dropout = nn.Dropout(drop_p)
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(hidden_dim * 3, hidden_dim)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, 1)
        self.sig = nn.Sigmoid()

    def forward(self, x, hidden):
        x = self.emb(x)
        out, hidden = self.lstm(x, hidden)

        last_hidden = out[:, -1, :]
        max_pool = torch.max(out, dim=1)[0]
        mean_pool = torch.mean(out, dim=1)

        features = torch.cat([last_hidden, max_pool, mean_pool], dim=1)
        features = self.dropout(torch.relu(self.fc1(features)))
        probs = self.sig(self.fc2(features))
        return probs, hidden

    def init_hidden(self, batch_size):
        weight = next(self.parameters()).data
        return (
            weight.new(self.num_layers, batch_size, self.hidden_dim).zero_().to(DEVICE),
            weight.new(self.num_layers, batch_size, self.hidden_dim).zero_().to(DEVICE)
        )

print("Concat pooling classifier ready.")

Concat pooling classifier ready.
In [14]:
# Bonus Upgrade 3: Fine-tune LM then the Classifier

# Prepare data for LM fine-tuning
text_corpus = " ".join(X_train[:3000])
encoded_corpus = np.array([char2int.get(c, 0) for c in text_corpus])

def run_lm_finetuning(model, data, epochs=1, lr=5e-4):
    model.train()
    opt = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=lr)
    loss_fn = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()

    for epoch in range(epochs):
        h = model.init_hidden(128)
        for x, y in get_batches(data, 128, 100):
            x, y = torch.LongTensor(x).to(DEVICE), torch.LongTensor(y).to(DEVICE)

            h = tuple(v.detach() for v in h)
            opt.zero_grad()

            logits, h = model(x, h)
            loss = loss_fn(logits, y.view(-1))
            loss.backward()
            nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), 5.0)
            opt.step()
        print(f"LM Fine-tune | Epoch {epoch+1} | Loss: {loss.item():.4f}")

def train_step(model, loader, opt, crit):
    model.train()
    total_loss, total = 0, 0
    for x, y in loader:
        x, y = x.to(DEVICE), y.to(DEVICE).float()
        h = model.init_hidden(x.size(0))

        out, _ = model(x, h)
        loss = crit(out.squeeze(), y)

        opt.zero_grad()
        loss.backward()
        opt.step()

        total_loss += loss.item() * y.size(0)
        total += y.size(0)
    return total_loss / total

#Adapt the language model to our specific subset
run_lm_finetuning(char_model_upgrade, encoded_corpus)

#Setup the classifier with the fine-tuned backbone
clf = CharSentimentClassifierConcat(char_model_upgrade, drop_p=0.4).to(DEVICE)
criterion = nn.BCELoss()

# frozen backbone
for p in clf.emb.parameters(): p.requires_grad = False
for p in clf.lstm.parameters(): p.requires_grad = False

warmup_opt = torch.optim.Adam(filter(lambda p: p.requires_grad, clf.parameters()), lr=1e-3)
print(" Stage 1: Classifier Warmup")
for _ in range(1):
    loss = train_step(clf, char_train_loader, warmup_opt, criterion)
    print(f"Warmup Loss: {loss:.4f} | Val Acc: {get_char_acc(clf, char_val_loader):.4f}")

#Full model fine-tuning with differential learning rates
for p in clf.parameters(): p.requires_grad = True

# Group parameters for different LRs (ULMFiT style)
full_opt = torch.optim.Adam([
    {'params': clf.emb.parameters(), 'lr': 1e-4},
    {'params': clf.lstm.parameters(), 'lr': 2e-4},
    {'params': clf.fc1.parameters(), 'lr': 1e-3},
    {'params': clf.fc2.parameters(), 'lr': 1e-3}
])

print(" Stage 2: End-to-End Fine-tuning")
for epoch in range(2):
    loss = train_step(clf, char_train_loader, full_opt, criterion)
    print(f"Epoch {epoch+1} | Loss: {loss:.4f} | Val Acc: {get_char_acc(clf, char_val_loader):.4f}")

# Final Evaluation
char_upgraded_test_acc = get_char_acc(clf, char_test_loader)
print(f"\nFinal Test Accuracy (Upgraded): {char_upgraded_test_acc:.4f}")

LM Fine-tune | Epoch 1 | Loss: 1.8275
 Stage 1: Classifier Warmup
Warmup Loss: 0.6946 | Val Acc: 0.5435
 Stage 2: End-to-End Fine-tuning
Epoch 1 | Loss: 0.6612 | Val Acc: 0.6621
Epoch 2 | Loss: 0.5936 | Val Acc: 0.7153

Final Test Accuracy (Upgraded): 0.7214
In [15]:
# Bonus Upgrade Comparison
df_bonus_upgrade_compare = pd.DataFrame({
    "Model Version": ["Original Part C", "Upgraded Part C"],
    "Test Accuracy": [char_test_acc, char_upgraded_test_acc],
    "Upgrade Notes": [
        "Frozen pretrained char LM + last hidden state classifier",
        "LM fine-tuning + unfrozen backbone + concat pooling classifier"
    ]
})

df_bonus_upgrade_compare["Delta vs Original"] = df_bonus_upgrade_compare["Test Accuracy"] - char_test_acc
display(df_bonus_upgrade_compare)
Model Version Test Accuracy Upgrade Notes Delta vs Original
0 Original Part C 0.502000 Frozen pretrained char LM + last hidden state ... 0.000000
1 Upgraded Part C 0.721375 LM fine-tuning + unfrozen backbone + concat po... 0.219375
In [ ]:
'''
PROVIDE YOUR ANSWER BELOW

I started by running a baseline on Part C of the problem using a frozen pre-trained character-level
language model and a classifier based on the final hidden state of the model. This baseline had a test accuracy of
0.5020, which was slightly better than a random guess.

Then, I implemented an upgraded version of this model, which was based on ULMFiT as suggetsed in task 4.
This had three parts:
1. Fine-tuning of the character-level language model on a larger subset of the IMDB data,
2. Warming up of the classifier while keeping the pre-trained backbone frozen
3. Unfreezing of the entire model and fine-tuning with differential learning rates, together with a concat-pooling classifier.

This upgraded method increased the accuracy during the validation stage from 0.5435 during the
classifier warm-up to 0.7153 after the end-to-end fine-tuning. The final upgraded accuracy for the
test stage was 0.7214. The accuracy is higher than the original Part C accuracy, which was 0.5020.
Therefore, the improvement indicates that the upgrades to the model.

Although there is an improvement, the upgraded character-level model did not perform as well as Part A,
with an accuracy of 0.8496, and Part B, with an accuracy of 0.9275. Therefore, even significant improvement
exists to the weak baseline model, the character-level model is not as effective as it should be.



'''

(2) reupload your file to the temporary Google Colab storage (you can access the temporary storage from the tab to the left)

Assignment Grading Rubric¶

The grading of the assignment will be based on the following categories:

(1) 10 Pt - EXPLORATORY QUESTIONS These are basic questions that in most cases can be answered without requiring a fully working and trained neural network model. For example, data loading, processing and visualization, summary statistics, data exploration, model and training setup, etc.

(2) 10 Pt - MODEL Student has successfully implemented all the required neural network models and has demonstrated successful training of the model without any errors.

(3) 10 Pt - RESULT Students are evaluated based on the results achieved in comparison to the expected results of the assignment.

(4) 10 Pt - DISCUSSION QUESTIONS Student demonstrated understanding beyond the basic exploratory questions, can answer some of the more challenging questions, and provide arguments for their model selection decisions.

(5) 10 Pt - COMMUNICATION Student has provided a quality submission that is easy to read without too many unnecessary output statements that distract the reading of the document. The code has been well commented and all the answers are communicated clearly and concisely.

(6) 5 Pt - BONUS Student has completed the assignment and has taken on the challenging bonus tasks listed in PART C. The student has demonstrated a good understanding of all aspects of the assignment and has exceeded expectations for the assignment.

TOTAL GRADE = _____ of 50 Pts
